stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to his GP for a pre-kinder health check. He is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile for height and weight, fully immunised, and has no exercise intolerance, syncope, cyanosis, or breathlessness on play. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital heart disease. He has had no recent sore throats, no joint pains, and no rashes. On examination he is pink and well perfused with normal femoral pulses, no thrill, a quiet S1 and S2 with no fixed splitting, and a soft murmur best heard at the lower left sternal edge while he is supine. The murmur has a distinctly musical, vibratory or twangy quality, occupies early to mid-systole only, is grade 2 out of 6 in intensity, and softens considerably when he sits up and almost disappears with the Valsalva manoeuvre.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96 bpm, RR 20 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 95/58 mmHg right arm
data_table.Examination: Pink, no clubbing or cyanosis, femoral pulses equal to brachials, apex non-displaced, no thrill, no diastolic component, no radiation to neck or axilla
data_table.Growth: Height and weight on 50th centile, head circumference appropriate

lead_in: What is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Still's vibratory innocent murmur
options.rationale: A musical or vibratory grade 1 to 2 early systolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge in a well child aged 2 to 7 years, which softens with Valsalva and on standing, is the classic Still's murmur as per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Heart murmurs in children

options.text: Venous hum
options.rationale: A venous hum is a continuous low-pitched murmur heard infraclavicularly that abolishes with neck turning or supine positioning; this systolic-only murmur at the lower left sternal edge is not consistent, per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs.

options.text: Pulmonary flow murmur
options.rationale: Pulmonary flow murmurs are best heard at the upper left sternal edge and are typically blowing rather than musical; the lower left sternal edge location and vibratory quality favour Still's per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs.

options.text: Carey-Coombs mid-diastolic murmur
options.rationale: Carey-Coombs is a soft mid-diastolic apical murmur of acute rheumatic carditis; this is systolic and the child has no Jones features, per RHDAustralia guideline.

options.text: Small restrictive ventricular septal defect
options.rationale: A small VSD produces a harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge, often with a thrill, and does not soften with Valsalva; the musical quality and dynamic changes favour Still's per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs.

explanation.summary: Innocent murmurs are common in well children. Still's is the prototypical vibratory early-systolic LLSE murmur of 2 to 7 year olds. Innocent murmurs are soft (grade 1 to 2), systolic only, position-dependent, and accompanied by normal pulses, S2, growth, and no symptoms.
key_points: Innocent murmurs: soft, systolic, sensitive to position, single S2, symptomless, small (grade 1 to 2)
Still's: vibratory LLSE, 2 to 7 years
Venous hum: continuous infraclavicular, abolished supine
Pulmonary flow: ULSE, blowing, often in adolescents
Carey-Coombs: mid-diastolic apical, ARF carditis
explanation.pearls: Red flags warranting echo: diastolic murmur, grade 3 or louder, harsh quality, abnormal S2, symptoms, abnormal pulses, syndromic features, or family history of sudden death.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Heart murmurs in children

stem: An 8-month-old boy with known unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot presents to a regional ED with his mother. He is awaiting elective surgical repair scheduled in 6 weeks. This morning after a feed he became inconsolable, cried vigorously, and then turned a dusky blue colour with deep, rapid breathing. The episode has been going on for around 10 minutes by the time he reaches the resuscitation cubicle. He has not vomited, has had no recent fever, and there is no diarrhoea or reduced wet nappies. Baseline SpO2 is usually 88 to 92% per cardiology letters. On arrival he is markedly cyanosed centrally with extremities mottled, breathing deeply at 70 per minute, and the previously loud harsh ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge is now barely audible. He has good IV access from the ambulance.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 188 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 62% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, BP 78/46 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Centrally cyanosed, irritable but rousable, deep Kussmaul-like respirations, soft 1/6 ESM ULSE (previously 4/6), CRT 4 seconds peripherally
data_table.Background: Known TOF, awaiting repair, baseline SpO2 88 to 92%, no prior tet spells documented

lead_in: After applying high-flow oxygen, which single next intervention is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid push followed by saline flush
options.rationale: Adenosine treats SVT, not a hypercyanotic spell; the rhythm here is sinus tachycardia driven by right-to-left shunting through the VSD per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot.

options.text: Place the infant in the knee-chest position and give IV morphine 0.1 mg/kg
options.rationale: First-line management of a tet spell is knee-chest positioning to raise systemic vascular resistance plus IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg to abolish hyperpnoea and reduce infundibular spasm, as per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot and AHA scientific statement.

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg/kg to offload the right ventricle
options.rationale: Frusemide reduces preload and worsens right-to-left shunting in a tet spell; volume expansion not depletion is required per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot.

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min
options.rationale: PGE1 maintains ductal patency in duct-dependent neonatal lesions; at 8 months the duct is closed and PGE1 has no role, per BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg bolus before any other intervention
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for documented severe acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluids and morphine have failed; it is not the next step, per AHA scientific statement.

explanation.summary: A tet spell is a sudden increase in right-to-left shunt through the VSD due to infundibular spasm or a drop in SVR. The murmur softens because pulmonary flow falls. Stepwise management: calm the child, knee-chest position, 100% oxygen, IV fluid bolus 10 to 20 mL/kg, IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg, then IV propranolol 0.1 mg/kg or phenylephrine if refractory.
key_points: Murmur softens during spell (less pulmonary flow)
Knee-chest raises SVR, reducing right-to-left shunt
Morphine reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm
Avoid agents that drop SVR or preload
Refractory: propranolol, phenylephrine, intubation, ECMO, urgent surgery
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockers are used both acutely and as a bridge to surgery to prevent spells; never give inotropes that increase contractility (worsens infundibular spasm).

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

stem: A 10-week-old term male infant is brought to a paediatric clinic by his parents because of poor feeding and slow weight gain. They report that he sweats profusely during breastfeeds, often pauses to catch his breath after a few sucks, and takes more than 40 minutes per feed. He has dropped from the 50th to the 9th weight centile over 6 weeks. There has been no cyanosis, no syncope, and no fevers. At his newborn baby check a soft murmur had been noted and an echocardiogram at 4 weeks showed a moderately large perimembranous ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt and mild left atrial and ventricular dilatation. He is now visibly tachypnoeic at rest, with intercostal recession, a hyperdynamic precordium, a 3/6 harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge, and a palpable liver edge 3 cm below the costal margin.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 170 bpm, RR 64 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, BP 82/48 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Tachypnoeic with subcostal and intercostal recession, hyperdynamic precordium, 3/6 PSM LLSE with thrill, hepatomegaly 3 cm, mild bibasal crackles
data_table.Growth: Weight dropped from 50th to 9th centile since birth
data_table.Echo (4 weeks): Moderate perimembranous VSD, left-to-right shunt, mild LA and LV dilatation, normal LV function

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial medical management?

options.text: Reassure parents and review in 3 months expecting spontaneous closure
options.rationale: Spontaneous closure is common only for small muscular VSDs; this infant has failure to thrive and heart failure requiring treatment, per RCH CPG: Heart failure in children.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children

options.text: Commence oral propranolol 0.5 mg/kg three times daily and high-energy formula
options.rationale: Beta-blockers are not first-line in infant left-to-right shunt failure; diuretic plus afterload reduction is standard, per BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence oral frusemide 1 mg/kg twice daily and an ACE inhibitor with high-energy feeds, and refer for surgical closure if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Standard medical therapy of large VSD with heart failure in infancy is loop diuretic plus ACE inhibitor for afterload reduction plus calorie-dense feeds, with surgical closure typically before 6 to 12 months if failure to thrive or pulmonary hypertension persists, per RCH CPG and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children
BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: PGE1 is used in duct-dependent lesions; a VSD with left-to-right shunt does not depend on the duct, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence oxygen therapy at 2 L/min via nasal prongs for resting tachypnoea
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen lowers pulmonary vascular resistance and increases left-to-right shunting, worsening pulmonary overcirculation in a VSD, per RCH CPG: Heart failure in children.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children

explanation.summary: Symptomatic large VSD presents at 6 to 12 weeks as pulmonary vascular resistance falls and left-to-right shunt increases. Features include tachypnoea, sweating with feeds, hepatomegaly and failure to thrive. Medical management is loop diuretic plus ACE inhibitor plus high-energy feeds, with surgical closure for ongoing failure to thrive, pulmonary hypertension, or large unrestricted shunt before fixed pulmonary vascular disease develops.
key_points: Murmur is harsh pansystolic LLSE, often with thrill
Frusemide 1 to 2 mg/kg BD plus ACE inhibitor (captopril or enalapril)
Avoid supplemental O2 (drops PVR, worsens shunt)
Surgery before fixed pulmonary vascular disease
Small muscular VSDs often close spontaneously
explanation.pearls: Symptoms typically appear at 4 to 8 weeks because pulmonary vascular resistance must fall before left-to-right shunting becomes haemodynamically significant.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children
BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

stem: A preterm male infant born at 27 weeks gestation is now day 6 of life on the neonatal intensive care unit. Birthweight was 950 g. He was initially ventilated for surfactant-deficient lung disease and is currently on synchronised intermittent mandatory ventilation with FiO2 0.35 and mean airway pressure 9 cm H2O. Over the past 24 hours he has required escalating ventilatory support, has developed widening pulse pressure, bounding femoral pulses, and a continuous murmur at the upper left sternal edge. He has been passing only 0.8 mL/kg/h of urine despite normal fluid intake. A formal echocardiogram performed this morning confirms a haemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus with a 2.4 mm internal diameter, left-to-right shunt, left atrium to aortic root ratio of 1.8, and reversed end-diastolic flow in the post-ductal descending aorta. Renal function and platelet count are within accepted neonatal limits and there is no evidence of necrotising enterocolitis or active intraventricular haemorrhage.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR per vent 50, SpO2 92% on FiO2 0.35, BP 48/22 mmHg (wide pulse pressure), MAP 31 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bounding peripheral pulses, hyperdynamic precordium, continuous machinery murmur ULSE, lungs with diffuse crackles
data_table.Echo: PDA 2.4 mm, LA:Ao 1.8, reversed end-diastolic flow descending aorta, no other structural disease
data_table.Bloods: Platelets 220 x10^9/L, creatinine 55 micromol/L, urine output 0.8 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: PGE1 is used to keep the duct open in duct-dependent lesions; here the PDA is causing pulmonary overcirculation and needs closure, per RCH NICU PDA guideline.
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus

options.text: Restrict fluids to 100 mL/kg/day and continue conservative management with no pharmacological closure
options.rationale: Conservative management is reasonable for non-haemodynamically significant PDA, but this infant has clear haemodynamic significance with escalating ventilation, wide pulse pressure, and reversed diastolic flow, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

options.text: Refer immediately for surgical ligation as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Surgical ligation is reserved for failure or contraindication of medical therapy; pharmacological closure is first-line when not contraindicated, per RCH NICU CPG: PDA.
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus

options.text: Administer an ibuprofen or indomethacin course for pharmacological PDA closure
options.rationale: In preterm infants with haemodynamically significant PDA and no contraindication (NEC, active IVH, renal failure, thrombocytopenia), a course of ibuprofen or indomethacin is first-line pharmacological closure, per RCH NICU CPG: PDA.
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus
BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

options.text: Commence oral paracetamol 15 mg/kg six-hourly indefinitely as definitive closure agent
options.rationale: Paracetamol is an emerging second-line option, typically as a 3 to 7 day course when COX inhibitors are contraindicated; it is not given indefinitely or as routine first-line, per BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

explanation.summary: Haemodynamically significant PDA in preterm neonates causes pulmonary overcirculation, prolonged ventilation, IVH, NEC and BPD. Echo criteria for significance include diameter > 1.5 mm, LA:Ao > 1.4, reversed diastolic flow in descending aorta. First-line closure is a COX-inhibitor course (ibuprofen or indomethacin) or paracetamol if contraindicated, with surgical ligation if pharmacological therapy fails. Term PDA usually closes spontaneously within 72 hours; persistent term PDA is managed by elective device closure beyond infancy.
key_points: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg then 5 mg/kg at 24 and 48 h is now preferred (less renal toxicity than indomethacin)
Contraindications: NEC, IVH, oliguria, thrombocytopenia, sepsis
Surgical ligation if medical fails and PDA remains significant
Term infants: catheter device closure beyond infancy
Echo confirms significance before treatment
explanation.pearls: Indomethacin reduces IVH rates but at the cost of more transient oliguria; ibuprofen has become the more widely used agent in Australian NICUs.

sources.label: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus
BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

stem: A 6-day-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his mother. He was born by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with birthweight 3.4 kg, passed all routine newborn checks, and was discharged at 36 hours of life. Over the past 24 hours he has fed poorly, has been increasingly lethargic, and his mother noticed his legs looking mottled and feeling cool while his upper body remained warm. He has had no fevers and no vomiting. On arrival he is pale, lethargic, and grunting. Brachial pulses are easily palpable bilaterally, but the femoral pulses are absent. Pre-ductal SpO2 on the right hand is 96%, while post-ductal SpO2 on the right foot is 84%. Blood pressure in the right arm is 78/44 mmHg and in the right leg is 42/28 mmHg, giving a clear upper-to-lower limb gradient. The liver is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin and there is metabolic acidosis on the venous blood gas.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 deg C, HR 180 bpm, RR 70 /min, BP arm 78/44 / leg 42/28 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Absent femoral pulses, brachial pulses present, mottled cool legs, warm upper body, hepatomegaly 3 cm, single loud S2
data_table.VBG: pH 7.18, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, HCO3 12 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting transfer?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 1 mg/kg and fluid restriction to 60 mL/kg/day
options.rationale: Diuresis is adjunctive for pulmonary congestion but will not address the lethal ductal closure underlying duct-dependent systemic circulation, per RCH CPG: Neonatal cardiology.

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg and oral feeds withheld pending surgical review
options.rationale: Vitamin K does not treat duct closure; immediate PGE1 is required to reopen the duct and maintain systemic perfusion, per BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

options.text: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min titrated to SpO2 above 95%
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen further constricts the ductus arteriosus and can accelerate cardiovascular collapse in duct-dependent systemic circulation; target SpO2 should be lower (around 85 to 95%), per RCH CPG.

options.text: Intravenous adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia
options.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia described; the differential pulses and saturation differential are diagnostic of coarctation, not SVT, per RCH CPG: SVT.

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion at 0.05 microg/kg/min via secure IV access
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation from coarctation or interrupted aortic arch presents at day 5 to 10 with shock as the duct closes; PGE1 at 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min reopens the duct and is the immediate life-saving therapy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal cardiology and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

explanation.summary: Critical coarctation presents at the end of the first week as the duct closes, producing shock, absent femoral pulses, upper-to-lower limb BP gradient, pre/post-ductal SpO2 differential, and metabolic acidosis. Immediate management is PGE1 infusion (commonly 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min titrated), correction of acidosis, careful fluid resuscitation, and urgent transfer for surgical repair. Side effects of PGE1 include apnoea, fever, hypotension, and flushing; be prepared to intubate.
key_points: Duct-dependent systemic: coarctation, interrupted arch, critical AS, HLHS
PGE1 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min IV
Apnoea is a common side effect; have airway ready
Avoid hyperoxia (constricts duct)
Definitive: surgical or catheter repair
explanation.pearls: If the duct closes before diagnosis, the infant can deteriorate from well to moribund within hours; any unwell neonate in the first 2 weeks needs femoral pulses and 4-limb BPs.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

stem: A 5-month-old previously well girl is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance because of poor feeding and pallor for 3 hours. Her mother reports that she felt the baby's chest racing earlier this morning and noticed she was breathing fast and looking sweaty during a feed. There has been no fever, no diarrhoea, no rash, and no preceding viral illness over the past week. On arrival to resus she is pale, mottled, and lethargic but responsive to voice. Femoral and brachial pulses are present but extremely rapid and impossible to count manually. A 12-lead ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 285 bpm with no clearly visible P waves and a constant RR interval. There are no delta waves on the resting trace once an experienced cardiologist reviews her baseline ECG from a previous well-child check. She has good peripheral IV access placed by paramedics en route. She is not yet hypotensive and remains alert.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 285 bpm (ECG), RR 60 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 76/48 mmHg
data_table.ECG: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia, rate 285, no visible P waves, constant RR, no QRS widening
data_table.Examination: Pale, mottled, responsive, CRT 3 seconds, no hepatomegaly yet, lungs clear

lead_in: After applying continuous cardiac monitoring and defibrillation pads, what is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Apply a bag of crushed ice wrapped in a cloth to the face for up to 30 seconds while preparing IV adenosine
options.rationale: In a haemodynamically stable infant with SVT, the diving reflex provoked by an ice bag to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre and can be attempted first while preparing adenosine, per RCH CPG: SVT and ANZCOR guideline 12.6.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

options.text: Immediate unsynchronised defibrillation at 4 J/kg
options.rationale: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for VF or pulseless VT; for unstable SVT the correct shock is synchronised cardioversion starting at 0.5 to 1 J/kg, per ANZCOR Guideline 12.6.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

options.text: Intravenous verapamil 0.1 mg/kg slow push
options.rationale: Verapamil is contraindicated in infants under 12 months due to risk of refractory hypotension and cardiac arrest, per AMH Children's Dosing Companion.

options.text: Oral propranolol 1 mg/kg as a stat dose
options.rationale: Oral beta-blockade is used for chronic prevention of recurrent SVT, not for acute termination of an episode in an unwell infant, per RCH CPG: SVT.

options.text: Carotid sinus massage on the right side for 10 seconds
options.rationale: Carotid sinus massage is not used in infants; ice to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre in this age group, per ANZCOR Guideline 12.6.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

explanation.summary: Infant SVT typically presents as poor feeding, pallor and tachypnoea with rates over 220 bpm and a narrow complex on ECG. In the stable infant: vagal manoeuvre (ice to face) then IV adenosine 0.1 mg/kg, repeat 0.2 mg/kg, then 0.3 mg/kg up to a single max of 12 mg, given rapidly into a large vein with saline flush. In the unstable infant proceed straight to synchronised DC cardioversion 0.5 to 1 J/kg, then 2 J/kg.
key_points: Stable: ice to face, then adenosine 0.1 then 0.2 mg/kg
Unstable: synchronised cardioversion 0.5 to 1 J/kg
Avoid verapamil under 12 months
Maintenance with propranolol or flecainide guided by cardiology
Check for WPW on sinus ECG after termination
explanation.pearls: Adenosine causes a brief, alarming asystolic pause; counsel the team, have monitor/defib pads on, and use a 3-way tap so the saline flush immediately follows the bolus.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

stem: A 15-year-old girl is referred to a paediatric cardiology outpatient clinic after two episodes of exertional syncope. The first occurred while sprinting at school athletics, with a brief loss of consciousness during which her friends say her limbs jerked for around 10 seconds before she came round confused for 30 seconds. The second was while jumping in a pool 6 weeks later. There is no history of typical post-ictal phase, tongue biting, or incontinence. A maternal uncle died suddenly in his 20s while swimming; the family was told it was drowning. She takes no regular medications and has never used a QT-prolonging drug. Examination is unremarkable with normal heart sounds, no murmur, and normal blood pressure. A resting 12-lead ECG at the clinic is shown to a paediatric electrophysiologist. Manual measurement and Bazett correction across multiple leads in the same patient give a QTc of 510 ms, with broad-based notched T-waves in the precordial leads.

data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm 72 bpm, QTc 510 ms (Bazett), broad-based notched T waves V2 to V4, no delta wave
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle sudden death in 20s while swimming, no other known cardiac disease
data_table.Triggers: Both episodes occurred during exertion (sprint, swimming)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassurance, electrolyte replacement, and follow-up ECG in 12 months
options.rationale: QTc above 480 ms with exertional syncope and a suggestive family history meets diagnostic criteria for LQTS; reassurance alone is inappropriate, per Heart Rhythm Society and AHA expert consensus.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Commence a non-selective beta-blocker (propranolol or nadolol), restrict competitive and aquatic sport, and arrange first-degree relative cascade ECG screening
options.rationale: LQTS with syncope is high-risk; non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol or nadolol) reduce events, exercise restriction is recommended particularly for swimming in LQT1, and first-degree relatives need cascade screening with ECG +/- genetic testing, per AHA/HRS consensus and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily for QT shortening
options.rationale: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-selective beta-blockers without QT-prolonging effect (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred, per AMH and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Immediate implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) implantation as first-line therapy
options.rationale: ICD is reserved for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification, per AHA/HRS consensus.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Commence levetiracetam after referral to a neurologist for presumed seizures
options.rationale: Although hypoxic limb jerking can mimic seizures, the exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point firmly to LQTS not epilepsy, per BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

explanation.summary: Diagnostic QTc cut-offs: a single resting QTc > 480 ms with unexplained syncope, or > 500 ms without symptoms, supports LQTS (Schwartz score also used). Management includes activity modification (especially swimming/competitive sport in LQT1), avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte derangement, non-selective beta-blockade (propranolol or nadolol), genetic testing, cascade family screening, and ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough events.
key_points: QTc > 480 ms with syncope or > 500 ms asymptomatic suggests LQTS
LQT1: exertion (swimming); LQT2: auditory/emotion; LQT3: rest/sleep
First-line: nadolol or propranolol
Avoid sotalol and other QT-prolonging drugs
Cascade screening of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: A sudden death in a young relative while swimming is classic for LQT1; warn the family about both medication and electrolyte triggers and provide a written drug-avoidance list.

sources.label: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed at a regional hospital after 10 days of migratory polyarthritis affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists in sequence over the preceding week. Each joint has been hot, painful, and swollen for 2 to 3 days before migrating to another joint, with dramatic and rapid resolution. She also had a sore throat 4 weeks ago that resolved without antibiotic treatment, and a throat swab taken at that time was not collected. She has no prior diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever and no known rheumatic heart disease on previous school-based screening echocardiograms performed at age 7. She has had no rash and no subcutaneous nodules detected on careful palpation of extensor surfaces. She has not had a documented fever during her current presentation, though her mother reports a febrile illness during the recent pharyngitis. There is no carditis clinically and a transthoracic echocardiogram performed today shows a structurally normal heart with no valvular regurgitation or pericardial effusion. ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with a PR interval of 220 ms (above the age-related upper limit). Anti-streptolysin O titre is 540 IU/mL and anti-DNase B is 380 U/mL, both elevated above the laboratory reference range. C-reactive protein is 60 mg/L, ESR is 65 mm/h, and a full blood count is otherwise unremarkable. The treating team is applying the 2020 RHDAustralia guideline using the criteria for high-risk populations.

data_table.Clinical: Migratory polyarthritis (knees, ankles, wrists), no carditis, no chorea, no subcutaneous nodules, no erythema marginatum, afebrile
data_table.Investigations: ASOT 540 IU/mL, anti-DNase B 380 U/mL, CRP 60 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/h, ECG PR 220 ms (above age threshold), echo normal
data_table.Background: Remote NT community, high-risk population, no prior ARF or RHD, sore throat 4 weeks ago

lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether this presentation meets the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria for an initial episode of acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population?

options.text: Does not meet criteria because she is afebrile and fever is mandatory for diagnosis
options.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with evidence of recent GAS infection, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because echocardiogram is normal and subclinical carditis is required
options.rationale: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence of carditis does not exclude ARF when other majors and minors are present, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets criteria for definite initial ARF: in high-risk populations polyarthritis is a major criterion and prolonged PR plus elevated acute phase reactants are minors, plus evidence of recent GAS infection
options.rationale: Under the 2020 RHDAustralia high-risk criteria, polyarthritis is a major; she has 1 major (polyarthritis) plus 3 minors (prolonged PR for age, raised CRP and ESR) plus serological evidence of recent GAS (raised ASOT and anti-DNase B), satisfying the 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence threshold for definite initial ARF, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets criteria only as probable ARF because monoarthritis is required in high-risk populations
options.rationale: In high-risk populations, monoarthritis or polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major and yields definite, not probable, ARF when criteria are otherwise met, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Does not meet criteria because anti-DNase B is non-specific and only ASOT counts as GAS evidence
options.rationale: Either ASOT or anti-DNase B (or both) is accepted as serological evidence of recent GAS infection, per RHDAustralia 2020.

explanation.summary: The 2020 RHDAustralia guideline differs from low-risk Jones in two key ways: in high-risk populations, monoarthritis and polyarthralgia are also majors (in addition to polyarthritis), and lower thresholds apply for fever (38 deg C) and ESR (30 mm/h). All groups need evidence of preceding GAS infection (ASOT, anti-DNase B, throat swab, or rapid antigen). Initial ARF requires 2 majors or 1 major + 2 minors plus GAS evidence.
key_points: High-risk Australia: ATSI, Maori/Pacific Islander, immigrants from endemic areas
Majors high-risk: carditis (incl. subclinical), polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Minors high-risk: fever >= 38 deg C, ESR >= 30 mm/h or CRP elevated, prolonged PR for age, monoarthralgia
Recurrence: 2 majors, OR 1 major + 2 minors, OR 3 minors + GAS evidence
Treat: benzathine penicillin G IM and secondary prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: Always check the population risk first; using low-risk criteria in a high-risk patient will under-diagnose and miss the opportunity for secondary prophylaxis.

stem: A 5-month-old male infant is referred to a paediatric ED with 6 days of unexplained fever to 39.5 deg C unresponsive to paracetamol. His mother reports increasing irritability and one day of red, cracked lips. He has had no diarrhoea, no cough, and no contact with sick household members. He has been investigated extensively in the community over 3 days. Urinalysis is sterile on culture. Blood cultures at 48 hours are negative, and a full septic screen including CSF is unremarkable. He has had no rash and no peripheral changes apart from puffy hands noted today. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection but no lymphadenopathy is palpable. C-reactive protein is 90 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/h, platelets 580 x10^9/L, ALT 65 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, white cell count 17 x10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, and urine microscopy shows 25 white cells per high-power field without organisms.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 175 bpm, RR 42 /min, BP 92/50 mmHg
data_table.Clinical features: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, red cracked lips, puffy hands, no rash, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: CRP 90 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/h, platelets 580 x10^9/L, WCC 17 with neutrophilia, ALT 65, albumin 28, sterile pyuria (25 WBC/hpf, no organisms)
data_table.Cultures: Blood, urine, CSF all negative at 48 h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and ibuprofen for presumed viral illness and review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Persistent fever > 5 days with classic Kawasaki features in an infant under 6 months mandates urgent assessment for coronary artery disease, not discharge, per RCH CPG: Kawasaki disease.

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg for occult bacterial sepsis and continue cultures
options.rationale: Cultures have been persistently negative across multiple sites and antibiotics have not improved fever; Kawasaki disease must now be considered, per BMJ Best Practice: Kawasaki disease.

options.text: Request transthoracic echocardiogram to look for coronary artery aneurysms before any further treatment decisions
options.rationale: Echocardiogram is essential and should be arranged urgently, but in incomplete KD with supportive laboratory criteria treatment with IVIG should not be delayed pending echo, per AHA Kawasaki disease scientific statement.

options.text: Diagnose incomplete Kawasaki disease, treat with intravenous immunoglobulin 2 g/kg as a single infusion plus aspirin, and arrange transthoracic echocardiogram
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months commonly present with incomplete KD. Fever >= 5 days plus 2 to 3 principal features plus supportive labs (CRP >= 30 mg/L or ESR >= 40 mm/h, plus 3 or more of anaemia, platelets >= 450, albumin <= 30, raised ALT, WCC >= 15, sterile pyuria) supports incomplete KD; treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin, with urgent echo to assess coronaries, per AHA statement and RCH CPG.

options.text: Commence high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg as monotherapy
options.rationale: Steroids are an adjunct (particularly for high-risk patients or IVIG-resistant disease), not monotherapy; IVIG remains first-line, per AHA scientific statement: Kawasaki disease.

explanation.summary: Classic KD requires fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 principal features (conjunctival injection, oral changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy > 1.5 cm). Infants under 6 months and over 8 years commonly have incomplete disease with fewer features but a high risk of coronary artery aneurysms. The AHA algorithm allows diagnosis when fever >= 5 days plus 2 to 3 features plus supportive labs are present, or with abnormal echocardiogram. Treatment: IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin (high-dose anti-inflammatory then low-dose antiplatelet).
key_points: Incomplete KD: think in any infant under 6 months with fever > 7 days
Supportive labs: CRP >= 30, ESR >= 40, plus 3 of (anaemia, plt >= 450 after day 7, albumin <= 30, raised ALT, WCC >= 15, sterile pyuria)
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion within 10 days reduces aneurysm risk from 25% to under 5%
Aspirin (high-dose anti-inflammatory, then low-dose antiplatelet)
Echo at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 to 8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria is a classic and often missed clue; always check a urine in prolonged unexplained fever.

stem: A 36-hour-old term male newborn is reviewed on the postnatal ward at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. His mother is 39 years old and declined non-invasive prenatal testing and combined first-trimester screening during pregnancy; the antenatal morphology scan at 20 weeks was reported as normal with limited views of the cardiac four-chamber view. Birthweight was 3.1 kg at 39+1 weeks gestation. On clinical examination the baby has marked generalised hypotonia, brachycephaly with flattened occiput, upslanting palpebral fissures with bilateral epicanthic folds, small low-set ears with overfolded helices, a flat nasal bridge, a protruding tongue, a single palmar crease bilaterally, and a wide gap between the first and second toes. The midwife noted bilious green vomiting after the first three feeds and the abdomen is now distended with absent bowel sounds. A plain abdominal radiograph performed at 24 hours of life shows a classic double bubble appearance with no distal gas. There is a soft 2/6 systolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge with no thrill. Femoral pulses are easily palpable and equal bilaterally. He is not cyanotic and pre/post-ductal saturations are both 96%. Full blood count taken on capillary sample shows a white cell count of 24 x10^9/L with 35% blasts on the film, although the consultant haematologist notes the morphology and clinical context are most consistent with transient abnormal myelopoiesis. The neonatal team needs to prioritise the most urgent investigation in the first 24 hours.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Hypotonia, brachycephaly, upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthic folds, single palmar creases, sandal-gap toes
data_table.GI: Bilious vomiting, double-bubble on AXR, no distal gas
data_table.Cardiac: Soft 2/6 LLSE systolic murmur, femoral pulses present, no cyanosis
data_table.Haematology: WCC 24 x10^9/L, 35% blasts, morphology in keeping with TAM
data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 158, RR 50, SpO2 96% on room air

lead_in: Which single investigation is the highest priority in the newborn screening period for this infant?

options.text: Lumbar puncture and CSF cytology to exclude CNS involvement of leukaemia
options.rationale: Transient abnormal myelopoiesis usually self-resolves and does not require LP at presentation; cardiac and surgical assessment take priority, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

options.text: MRI brain to characterise dysmorphology and exclude structural CNS malformation
options.rationale: Routine MRI brain is not indicated as part of newborn Down syndrome assessment; the priority lies elsewhere, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

options.text: Hip ultrasound to screen for developmental dysplasia of the hip
options.rationale: Hip ultrasound is performed at 6 weeks (or earlier if clinical signs); it is not a 24-hour priority over cardiac and surgical assessment, per RCH CPG.

options.text: TSH and free T4 measurement to detect congenital hypothyroidism
options.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened via the newborn bloodspot at 48 to 72 hours and confirmatory venous testing if abnormal; it is important but lower priority than urgent cardiac assessment given duodenal atresia needs theatre, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

options.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram to define cardiac anatomy before surgical correction of duodenal atresia
options.rationale: Up to 50% of T21 newborns have a structural cardiac defect, commonly AVSD. The infant requires urgent laparotomy for duodenal atresia; defining cardiac anatomy by echo is essential before anaesthesia to anticipate haemodynamic risks and plan management, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome and BMJ Best Practice.

explanation.summary: All newborns with suspected or confirmed T21 require echocardiography within the first month (within days if a duct-dependent lesion is suspected or surgery is planned), karyotype confirmation, audiology, ophthalmology referral, thyroid screening on bloodspot, and FBC. With concurrent duodenal atresia requiring laparotomy and a murmur, urgent pre-operative echo is essential to characterise an AVSD or other lesion.
key_points: Up to 50% have CHD; AVSD is the commonest serious defect
Duodenal atresia in 5% of T21 newborns
TAM in 10%; usually self-resolves but 20% develop AML by age 5
Newborn workup: echo, karyotype, FBC, TSH, audiology, red reflex, hip US at 6 weeks
Lifelong: regular thyroid, coeliac, vision, hearing, OSA screening
explanation.pearls: Before anaesthesia in any T21 infant, you must know the cardiac anatomy and the C1-C2 stability for older children (atlanto-axial instability).

stem: A 2-day-old term female neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after the midwife noted persistent bilateral lower limb swelling and unusual neck skin folds. Birthweight was 2.8 kg at 39 weeks; the pregnancy was complicated by increased nuchal translucency at 12 weeks, but the parents declined further prenatal testing. On examination she has marked pedal and dorsal hand lymphoedema, a webbed posterior neck with redundant skin folds, a broad shield chest with widely spaced nipples, and a low posterior hairline. Right brachial pulse is normal but both femoral pulses feel weaker than the brachials and there is a 22 mmHg systolic differential between right arm and right leg cuff blood pressures. She is feeding well and not cyanosed. SpO2 pre-ductal is 97% and post-ductal is 95%. A genetic karyotype has been sent and is pending. The team must decide which finding warrants the most urgent investigation now.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Pedal lymphoedema, webbed neck, broad shield chest, widely spaced nipples, low posterior hairline
data_table.Cardiovascular: Right brachial pulse normal, both femoral pulses weaker, right arm BP 78/46 vs right leg 56/30 mmHg (22 mmHg systolic gradient)
data_table.Vitals: T 36.7, HR 148, RR 46, SpO2 97 pre-ductal / 95 post-ductal

lead_in: Which single feature in this newborn most urgently warrants an immediate echocardiogram?

options.text: Weakened femoral pulses and a systolic blood pressure differential between right arm and right leg, suggesting coarctation of the aorta
options.rationale: Approximately 30% of Turner syndrome neonates have congenital heart disease, of which coarctation and bicuspid aortic valve are commonest. Weak femorals and an upper-to-lower limb BP gradient are clinical signs of coarctation that mandate urgent echo to assess for duct-dependent systemic circulation, per RCH CPG: Turner syndrome and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Pedal lymphoedema, which suggests a renovascular cause requiring urgent renal artery Doppler
options.rationale: Lymphoedema is due to congenital lymphatic dysplasia and does not require renal artery imaging; cardiac assessment is the priority, per BMJ Best Practice: Turner syndrome.

options.text: Webbed neck, which mandates urgent cervical spine MRI for atlanto-axial instability
options.rationale: Atlanto-axial instability is associated with Down syndrome, not Turner syndrome; webbed neck reflects cystic hygroma resolution and does not require cervical MRI, per RCH CPG.

options.text: Widely spaced nipples, which require urgent breast ultrasound to exclude congenital absence of breast tissue
options.rationale: Widely spaced nipples are a dysmorphic feature and not an imaging indication in the newborn period, per BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Low posterior hairline, which warrants urgent thyroid ultrasound for ectopic thyroid
options.rationale: Thyroid disease in Turner syndrome is monitored by TFTs over time, not via newborn ultrasound based on hairline, per RCH CPG: Turner syndrome.

explanation.summary: Turner syndrome (45,X and variants) classically presents at birth with lymphoedema and webbed neck (residual cystic hygroma) or later with short stature and primary ovarian failure. Congenital heart disease occurs in 25 to 45%, predominantly bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation, with aortic dissection risk in adulthood. All confirmed or suspected Turner syndrome patients need echo at diagnosis, with periodic surveillance lifelong.
key_points: 30% of Turner neonates have CHD; BAV and coarctation predominate
Newborn signs: lymphoedema, webbed neck, weak femorals
Later: short stature, delayed puberty, primary ovarian failure
Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney) in 30%
Lifelong cardiac surveillance for aortic dilatation
explanation.pearls: Any female newborn with unexplained pedal lymphoedema should have femoral pulses checked carefully and a karyotype sent.

stem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to a general paediatric clinic for assessment of failure to thrive and feeding difficulties. Her parents describe her as extremely friendly, overly chatty for her age, with no stranger anxiety; she will happily engage with anyone she meets. On examination she has periorbital fullness, a short upturned nose with a broad nasal tip, a long philtrum, full cheeks, and a wide mouth with full lips. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal edge with radiation to both carotids and a palpable suprasternal thrill. Blood pressure in the right arm is 105/60 mmHg and in the left arm is 88/52 mmHg, with a clear right-arm hypertension. Calcium is 2.85 mmol/L on a fasted venous sample, ionised calcium 1.42 mmol/L, with normal albumin and PTH appropriately suppressed at 0.6 pmol/L. Renal ultrasound shows mild bilateral nephrocalcinosis. She is on age-appropriate solids and has had no excess vitamin D supplementation.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Periorbital fullness, short upturned nose, long philtrum, full cheeks, wide mouth, full lips
data_table.Cardiac: 3/6 ESM at right upper sternal edge radiating to carotids, suprasternal thrill, R arm BP > L arm BP
data_table.Biochemistry: Ca 2.85 mmol/L, iCa 1.42 mmol/L, PTH 0.6 pmol/L (suppressed), albumin normal

lead_in: What is the single most likely underlying diagnosis?

options.text: Noonan syndrome
options.rationale: Noonan syndrome features pulmonary stenosis (not supravalvular AS), hypertelorism with downslanting fissures and webbed neck, without supravalvular AS or hypercalcaemia, per BMJ Best Practice: Noonan syndrome.

options.text: Williams (Williams-Beuren) syndrome
options.rationale: Elfin facies, supravalvular aortic stenosis (right upper sternal edge ESM radiating to carotids with right-arm hypertension and a Coanda effect), infantile hypercalcaemia with suppressed PTH, nephrocalcinosis, and an overfriendly cocktail-party personality are classic for Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion including ELN), per RCH CPG: Williams syndrome and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: DiGeorge (22q11.2 deletion) syndrome
options.rationale: 22q11.2 deletion typically presents with conotruncal cardiac defects (truncus, IAA type B, TOF), hypocalcaemia from parathyroid hypoplasia, and immunodeficiency, not hypercalcaemia or supravalvular AS, per BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Down syndrome (trisomy 21)
options.rationale: T21 features brachycephaly, upslanting fissures, single palmar crease, AVSD, and hypotonia; it does not produce supravalvular AS or infantile hypercalcaemia, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

options.text: Turner syndrome (45,X)
options.rationale: Turner syndrome features bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation rather than supravalvular AS, and is associated with lymphoedema, webbed neck and short stature, not hypercalcaemia or elfin facies, per BMJ Best Practice.

explanation.summary: Williams syndrome arises from a contiguous gene microdeletion at 7q11.23 (including ELN). The clinical triad includes elfin facies, cardiovascular disease (especially supravalvular AS, peripheral pulmonary stenosis), and infantile hypercalcaemia. Behaviour is characteristically overfriendly with relative strength in expressive language despite intellectual disability. The Coanda effect in supravalvular AS preferentially streams flow into the innominate artery, producing higher right than left arm blood pressure.
key_points: 7q11.23 microdeletion; FISH or microarray confirms
Supravalvular AS in 70%; surveillance lifelong
Hypercalcaemia in infancy; avoid vitamin D supplements
Overfriendly personality with intellectual disability
Increased risk of hypertension, diabetes, thyroid disease in adulthood
explanation.pearls: Asymmetric arm BPs with right > left in an unwell toddler with developmental delay should prompt thoughts of supravalvular AS and Williams syndrome.

stem: A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur identified at preschool entry assessment. He was born at term by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery with a birthweight of 3.2 kg, but his height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on the 10th centile, despite a mid-parental height on the 50th centile. His developmental milestones have been mildly delayed: he sat unsupported at 9 months, walked unaided at 18 months, and now uses only 2 to 3 word phrases at age 3. He has not had any significant illnesses and has been fully immunised. On examination he has hypertelorism with downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck with low posterior hairline, and a pectus carinatum superiorly with pectus excavatum inferiorly. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge, loudest on inspiration, with a soft single S2 and no thrill; the murmur does not radiate to the carotids. There is no left ventricular heave and the apex is not displaced. Blood pressure is symmetric in both arms at 96/58 mmHg and femoral pulses are present and equal. Echocardiogram performed by the paediatric cardiology team reveals a thickened, dysplastic pulmonary valve with a peak gradient of 45 mmHg, mild post-stenotic dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, and no left ventricular hypertrophy. Genetic testing for RASopathy genes via a multi-gene panel is pending.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck, pectus carinatum superior/excavatum inferior
data_table.Growth: Height 3rd centile, weight 10th centile, mid-parental height 50th centile
data_table.Cardiac: 3/6 ESM ULSE louder on inspiration, soft single S2, no thrill, no LVH, symmetric arm BPs
data_table.Echo: Dysplastic pulmonary valve, peak gradient 45 mmHg, no LV hypertrophy
data_table.Development: Walked 18 months, 2 to 3 word phrases, mild delay

lead_in: Which single genetic syndrome best fits this clinical picture?

options.text: Williams syndrome (7q11.23 microdeletion)
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features supravalvular aortic stenosis with a right-upper sternal edge murmur and elfin facies; this child has pulmonary stenosis and Noonan facies, per BMJ Best Practice: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Turner syndrome (45,X)
options.rationale: Turner syndrome occurs in phenotypic females with bicuspid AV and coarctation; this patient is male with pulmonary stenosis, per BMJ Best Practice: Turner syndrome.

options.text: Noonan syndrome, most often due to PTPN11 mutation in the RAS-MAPK pathway
options.rationale: Noonan syndrome features dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis (most common cardiac lesion in approximately 50%, with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in around 20%), short stature, hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, webbed neck, pectus deformity, and developmental delay; PTPN11 mutations account for about half of cases, per BMJ Best Practice: Noonan syndrome and RCH CPG.

options.text: Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation)
options.rationale: Marfan features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation, not short stature, webbed neck or pulmonary stenosis, per the revised Ghent criteria.

options.text: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)
options.rationale: T21 features upslanting fissures, brachycephaly, single palmar crease and AVSD, not pulmonary stenosis or downslanting fissures, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome.

explanation.summary: Noonan syndrome is an autosomal dominant RASopathy with variable expression. About 50% have pulmonary valve stenosis (often dysplastic and resistant to balloon valvuloplasty) and 20% have hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Short stature, characteristic facies (hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck), pectus deformity, mild intellectual disability, cryptorchidism, and bleeding diathesis are typical. Half the cases are due to PTPN11 mutations.
key_points: PTPN11 in approximately 50%; other RAS-MAPK genes (SOS1, RAF1, KRAS) account for most others
Dysplastic pulmonary stenosis is the cardiac hallmark
HCM in 20%; can present as infant heart failure or sudden cardiac death
Growth hormone is approved for short stature
Echo and ECG at diagnosis and periodically
explanation.pearls: A boy with the Turner phenotype is the classic teaching cue for Noonan syndrome (formerly mis-called male Turner).

stem: A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric cardiology clinic by his GP because of a tall, thin habitus and a recently identified murmur during a sports pre-participation examination. He has a height on the 99th centile and an arm span 7 cm greater than his height, with a reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio. He has long, slender fingers with positive Steinberg (thumb) and Walker-Murdoch (wrist) signs bilaterally. There is significant pectus excavatum requiring surgical consideration, a high-arched palate with dental crowding requiring orthodontic intervention, and mild scoliosis with Cobb angle 22 degrees on standing radiograph. An ophthalmology slit-lamp examination performed last month demonstrates bilateral superior ectopia lentis with mild myopia. Echocardiogram performed today shows aortic root dilatation at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.4 cm, corresponding to a Z-score of 3.8 indexed to body surface area, with mild aortic regurgitation and no evidence of dissection. The remaining cardiac anatomy is normal. His father died suddenly aged 38 from a presumed dissection of the ascending aorta while playing tennis; no formal post-mortem genetic testing was performed at the time. He currently plays competitive rugby league at school and trains four times per week with intensive resistance work. There is no history of joint dislocation or skin hyperextensibility. He has no learning difficulties, no recent syncope, and no exertional chest pain.

data_table.Skeletal: Height 99th centile, arm span > height by 7 cm, positive thumb (Steinberg) and wrist (Walker-Murdoch) signs, pectus excavatum, scoliosis (Cobb 22 deg), high-arched palate with dental crowding
data_table.Ocular: Bilateral superior ectopia lentis on slit lamp
data_table.Cardiovascular: Aortic root 4.4 cm at sinuses of Valsalva, Z-score 3.8, mild aortic regurgitation
data_table.Family history: Father died at 38 of presumed aortic dissection

lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure as athletic build, continue rugby league, and arrange repeat echo in 5 years
options.rationale: He meets revised Ghent criteria for Marfan syndrome (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score >= 2 with family history is more than sufficient); reassurance and continuation of high-impact contact sport is unsafe, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily and clear him to play competitive contact sport
options.rationale: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan syndrome; beta-blocker (atenolol or propranolol) or losartan is used to slow aortic dilatation, and contact and competitive sport should be avoided, per AHA scientific statement on aortopathies.
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Refer for immediate elective ascending aortic root replacement irrespective of Z-score
options.rationale: Elective root replacement is generally indicated at root diameter >= 5.0 cm in adults or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection or rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with a Z-score of 3.8 medical therapy and ongoing surveillance are first-line, per BMJ Best Practice: Marfan syndrome.

options.text: Commence atenolol or losartan, restrict competitive and contact sport, arrange annual echo and ophthalmology review, and offer first-degree relative cascade screening with FBN1 genetic testing
options.rationale: Marfan syndrome with aortic root dilatation requires medical therapy to slow root growth (beta-blocker or losartan, with evidence supporting both), restriction from competitive contact and isometric sport, annual echo surveillance, ophthalmology review for ectopia lentis and retinal complications, and cascade screening of first-degree relatives with FBN1 testing, per BMJ Best Practice and revised Ghent nosology.
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Treat with ramipril 5 mg daily and clear him for non-contact competitive swimming
options.rationale: Losartan (angiotensin receptor blocker), not ramipril, is the agent shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is also restricted, per BMJ Best Practice.

explanation.summary: The revised (2010) Ghent nosology diagnoses Marfan syndrome via various combinations of aortic root dilatation, ectopia lentis, FBN1 mutation, family history, and systemic features (systemic score >= 7). This patient meets criteria multiple ways. Management centres on slowing aortic dilatation with beta-blocker or losartan, lifestyle modification, annual echo, ophthalmology review for lens and retinal pathology, and prophylactic root replacement at threshold diameter (commonly >= 5.0 cm, or >= 4.5 cm with adverse features).
key_points: Revised Ghent (2010): aortic root Z >= 2 plus ectopia lentis is sufficient
Beta-blocker (atenolol) or losartan slows root growth
Restrict competitive contact and isometric sport
Annual echo plus ophthalmology
Cascade FBN1 testing of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: An aortic root Z-score in childhood is more meaningful than absolute diameter because of body size variability.

sources.label: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to a tertiary developmental paediatrician for assessment of intellectual disability and behavioural difficulties that have escalated over the past school year. His mother reports global developmental delay first noted at 18 months of age when he had only 2 words and was unable to follow simple instructions. He now attends a mainstream school with full-time aide support but has a recent Wechsler full-scale IQ of 58, placing him in the moderate intellectual disability range. He has marked attentional difficulties consistent with comorbid ADHD, hand-flapping when excited, persistently poor eye contact, perseverative speech with echolalia, and tactile hypersensitivity to clothing labels and certain food textures. He has had no seizures and has not lost previously acquired skills. On examination today he has a long narrow face with prominent forehead, large protruding ears, a high-arched palate, hyperextensible finger joints with thumbs that easily reach the volar forearm, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, and bilateral pes planus. Testicular examination shows symmetrically enlarged testes for prepubertal age, already at a Prader orchidometer volume of 6 mL bilaterally. There is no microcephaly, no neurocutaneous stigmata, and no dysmorphic facial midline defects. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle with mild intellectual disability and progressive ataxia who was diagnosed in his 60s with a late-onset intention tremor and gait disorder of unclear aetiology, and a maternal grandmother who experienced primary ovarian insufficiency with cessation of menses at age 36. Microarray comparative genomic hybridisation performed at age 4 was normal, as was a metabolic screen including very long chain fatty acids. The clinical geneticist is now considering targeted single-gene testing based on the clinical pattern.

data_table.Cognitive/behaviour: FSIQ 58, global delay, hand-flapping, poor eye contact, echolalia, tactile defensiveness, attentional difficulties
data_table.Examination: Long narrow face, prominent ears, high-arched palate, joint hypermobility, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, pes planus, prepubertal macroorchidism
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle late-onset tremor/ataxia diagnosed in 60s; maternal grandmother primary ovarian insufficiency at 36

lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects the most likely genetic diagnosis and the appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY); send a peripheral blood karyotype with chromosome enumeration
options.rationale: Klinefelter syndrome presents with tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism), gynaecomastia in adolescence, and milder cognitive impact, per BMJ Best Practice: Klinefelter syndrome.

options.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for 7q11.23 elastin deletion
options.rationale: Williams syndrome features elfin facies, supravalvular AS, hypercalcaemia and an overfriendly personality; family history and macroorchidism do not fit, per RCH CPG: Williams syndrome.

options.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; request DNA methylation analysis at 15q11-q13
options.rationale: Prader-Willi presents with neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia in childhood, obesity, hypogonadism (small testes), and short stature, with no macroorchidism or X-linked family pattern, per BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; request TSC1 and TSC2 sequencing
options.rationale: TSC presents with hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas, renal angiomyolipomas, cortical tubers and epilepsy; none of these features are present, per BMJ Best Practice: Tuberous sclerosis.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Tuberous sclerosis complex

options.text: Fragile X syndrome from a full FMR1 CGG repeat expansion (> 200); request targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation analysis (or Southern blot) since standard microarray does not detect trinucleotide expansions
options.rationale: The combination of moderate intellectual disability, autistic features, long face with prominent ears, joint hypermobility, post-pubertal macroorchidism (here pre-pubertal but already noted), a maternal uncle with FXTAS (fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome from a premutation) and maternal grandmother with FXPOI (fragile X-associated primary ovarian insufficiency) strongly indicates Fragile X (FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG); microarrays do not detect trinucleotide expansions and FMR1 targeted testing with methylation analysis is required, per RCH CPG: Fragile X and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

explanation.summary: Fragile X syndrome is the commonest inherited cause of intellectual disability and a leading single-gene cause of autism. It results from CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion (> 200) in the 5' untranslated region of FMR1 on Xq27.3, with methylation silencing the gene. Premutation carriers (55 to 200 repeats) can develop FXTAS (older men) or FXPOI (women under 40). Standard microarrays do not detect trinucleotide repeats; targeted FMR1 testing (PCR with methylation analysis or Southern blot) is required.
key_points: FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG, methylated, silenced
Long face, prominent ears, macroorchidism after puberty, joint hypermobility, autistic features
Premutation 55 to 200: FXTAS in older males, FXPOI in younger females
Microarray does not detect repeat expansions
Cascade testing across maternal line is essential
explanation.pearls: A combination of a boy with intellectual disability, an older male relative with late-onset ataxia/tremor, and a female relative with early menopause is essentially diagnostic of FMR1 pathology until proven otherwise.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome
